title: Minimal Surfaces and the Isoperimetric Inequality
speaker: Simon Brendle
abstract: The isoperimetric inequality has a long history in the geometry. In this lecture, we will discuss how the isoperimetric inequality can be generalized to submanifolds in Euclidean space. As a special case, we obtain a sharp isoperimetric inequality for minimal submanifolds of codimension at most 2, thereby answering a question going back to work of Carleman. The proof of that inequality is inspired by, but does not actually use, optimal transport.

proof_stages.label: Euclidean benchmark and Minkowski form
proof_stages.summary: The lecture fixes the sharp Euclidean isoperimetric constant, its equality model, and the equivalent tubular-neighborhood/Minkowski formulation that will guide the submanifold problem.

proof_stages.label: Minimal-submanifold problem and prior barriers
proof_stages.summary: The Euclidean inequality is reformulated for domains in minimal submanifolds, and earlier two-dimensional, area-minimizing, and Michael-Simon-type results are reviewed to isolate the sharp-constant and codimension difficulties.

proof_stages.label: Sharp mean-curvature inequality
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: boundary measure plus the \(L^1\)-norm of mean curvature controls the scale-invariant volume with a dimension/codimension constant, sharp in codimension two and hence in codimension one, with equality only for flat disks.

proof_stages.label: Unequal-dimensional transport blueprint
proof_stages.summary: A heuristic optimal-transport problem from the submanifold to an ambient ball is set up, yielding normal affine fibers and a mass-balance analogue of Monge-Ampere, and the auxiliary measure is calibrated by the flat-disk equality model.

proof_stages.label: ABP replacement and Neumann normalization
proof_stages.summary: To avoid regularity issues in the transport potential, the argument is recast through the ABP method: after scaling, a solvable linear Neumann problem is introduced and a transport-like map on the normal bundle is defined.

proof_stages.label: Surjectivity and Jacobian compression
proof_stages.summary: The ABP map is shown to cover the unit ball, its Jacobian is computed as a determinant involving the Hessian and second fundamental form, and the PDE plus AM-GM implies it is orientation-preserving and measure non-increasing.

proof_stages.label: Annular change-of-variables estimate
proof_stages.summary: Changing variables over a thin outer annulus that approximates the boundary-supported measure gives a lower bound for \(|\Sigma|\), and the normalization then converts this bound into the stated isoperimetric inequality.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is specialized to minimal submanifolds, converted into a submanifold Minkowski/tubular-neighborhood inequality and a monotonicity-formula case, and then related Riemannian, weighted, and volume-growth variants and limitations are discussed.

examples.name: Equal-dimensional quadratic optimal transport
examples.note: The familiar problem of transporting mass in \(\mathbb R^n\) with quadratic cost, or from a Euclidean domain to the unit ball via a gradient map, is used only to make the Kantorovich-potential and ABP/PDE pictures concrete before the unequal-dimensional construction.
examples.engagement_reason: he sets up the equal-dimensional quadratic-cost transport case, identifies the gradient-map potential and Monge–Ampère equation, and uses it as the model for the ABP/transport proof

examples.name: Naive Lebesgue measure under projection
examples.note: Trying ordinary Lebesgue measure on the higher-dimensional ball shows that orthogonal projection gives too little mass near the boundary, visibly motivating the boundary-concentrated auxiliary measure.
examples.engagement_reason: he tests the naive Lebesgue choice under orthogonal projection and explains why the pushed-forward measure puts too little weight near the boundary

examples.name: Point case of the tubular-neighborhood inequality
examples.note: Taking the set \(E\) to be a single point in the submanifold Minkowski inequality recovers the classical lower-density/monotonicity estimate for minimal submanifolds.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the tubular-neighborhood inequality to the special case where e is a point and derives the classical minimal-surface ball-volume lower bound

examples.name: Paraboloid with low asymptotic volume growth
examples.note: In response to a question on Riemannian ambient spaces, a paraboloid illustrates that if the asymptotic volume ratio is zero then the extension may give no nontrivial isoperimetric information.

examples.name: Cylindrical-end thought experiment
examples.note: A surface in an end opening only like a cylinder is used as a counterfactual to explain why a positive sharp coefficient would force too much volume growth, suggesting that the coefficient must vanish or the exponent must change.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through a cylinder-like end with a cap to show how a nonzero coefficient would contradict the inequality, using it to justify the zero/asymptotic-volume-growth case
